ETF-only 10% return / <15% volatility / drawdown-control study

Data boundary: 2004 warm-up, core comparison 2007-01-03 to 2021-12-31; every download stopped at 2022-01-01 exclusive.

Interpretation: historical CAGR is not a forward expected return. Short live ETF records and synthetic option marks are kept in separate evidence tiers. A buffer ETF is not principal-guaranteed, and a modeled collar is not an executable-chain backtest.

1 · Executive result

The frozen batch ran 104 rows, including 72 long-history ETF-rule portfolios. 7 long-history rows passed every frozen 9%+ return / 15% vol / -25% drawdown / cost / subperiod / concentration gate. The table below deliberately takes one representative per mechanism cluster; rows marked near-target or research-only are alternatives, not silent gate relaxations.

2 · Ten-plus relatively independent alternatives

Independence is enforced first by economic mechanism and portfolio construction, then checked with monthly return correlation and stress/regime fingerprints. Parameter neighbours remain counted as trials but do not earn extra candidate slots.

Six-month block-bootstrap uncertainty

These are resampling diagnostics, not forecasts. Short-history funds with fewer than 36 monthly observations are intentionally blank.

5 · Packaged ETF reality check

These are actual fund-price histories through 2021, net of each fund's embedded expenses. The evidence column and start date matter more than rank: several products existed for only two or three years before the cutoff and never saw the GFC.

6 · What was tested

  • Quarterly fixed US/global stock, Treasury, gold, commodity and sector allocations.
  • Weekly SPY/QQQ moving-average, 63-day volatility target, drawdown ladder and fast-exit/slow-reentry P&L controls.
  • Monthly fixed/inverse-vol/capped/trend-gated all-weather allocations.
  • Monthly GTAA, GEM, top-N cross-asset momentum, multi-horizon momentum and defensive momentum.
  • Monthly sector momentum, downside-deviation, barbell, downside-vol budget and Ulcer rules.
  • One frozen HYG/IEF credit-canary regime rule.
  • Seven synthetic SPY put/put-spread/collar overlays with conservative option-entry haircuts and financing.
  • Twenty-three packaged allocation, smart-beta, managed-futures, tail-risk, buffer, risk-parity and capital-efficient ETFs.

7 · Limits that matter

  • The common sample contains only one global financial crisis and one pandemic crash. Regime counts are small.
  • Today's ETF universe has survivorship and product-launch bias. The nine-sector universe is frozen, but delisted strategy ETFs are not represented.
  • Inverse volatility is a simple risk-balance proxy, not a full Bridgewater portfolio and not a forecast of growth/inflation surprises.
  • The SPY option module uses VIX and Black-Scholes with skew and spread haircuts. It lacks historical NBBO, exact strikes, American exercise, discrete dividends, tax and crisis liquidity.
  • Managed-futures ETFs are useful diversifiers, but their live pre-cutoff records are too short. Long index backfills are not treated as ETF performance.
  • Defined-outcome buffers apply point-to-point over an outcome period, have upside caps, can lose beyond the buffer, and are not guarantees.
  • No 2022 stock/bond inflation shock was used. A forward paper period is still required before allocation.

8 · Sources used to form testable hypotheses

  • Bridgewater — The All Weather Story
  • RPAR official strategy page
  • Cboe collar-index methodology
  • Cboe downside benchmark overview
  • Innovator defined-outcome education
  • AQR — A Century of Evidence on Trend-Following
  • NBER — Volatility Managed Portfolios
  • Faber tactical asset-allocation paper
  • Antonacci dual-momentum paper
  • WisdomTree NTSX official page
  • KraneShares KMLM official page
  • Amplify SWAN official page
  • Cambria TAIL official page
